item.title: The Daily Blogroll — Monday, 20 July 2026
item.description:
Shintar celebrates finishing a SWTOR Jedi Knight story, then dives into how often each class story got completed across her alts.

Kimimi makes a strong case for Atari 2600 Haunted House as real survival horror, with darkness, matches, and lightning doing the heavy lifting.

Nicole digs into the gloriously janky Brick Game, from weird packaging to fake variety and its very obvious Tetris obsession.

Wilhelm checks in on Fantasy Critic League as The Incident at Galley House waits on reviews and Denshattack! lands a strong early score.

The Chronicler gives Three Days of Gettysburg: Deluxe Edition an overview, spotlighting GMT’s lavish components, optional rules, and big Civil War scope.

Tobold says AI is great for cutting through SEO sludge and surfacing usable Pathfinder: Wrath of the Righteous build advice, hallucinations included.

WCRobinson recaps a personal push in Yu-Gi-Oh! Master Duel’s World Championship Qualifiers, covering prep, decks, performance, and lessons learned.

Andrew Plotkin flags a browser-playable return for The Fool and his Money, plus a neat little tour through puzzle-game preservation woes.

Tipa turns presidential history into a speedrun leaderboard, with Teddy Roosevelt’s record and 2028 contenders like AOC, Ossoff, and Tiffany Trump.

Brennan argues the IndieWeb needs a ladder, not a purity test, with easy platforms as stepping stones toward fuller ownership.

Dave Winer shares a WordPress example app for rss.chat and keeps pushing inbound RSS as a way to break social web lock-in.

Roger tunes up his Squarespace blog for Blaugust with a post counter and a much beefier, cleaner blogroll setup.

item.title: The Daily Blogroll — Sunday, 19 July 2026
item.description:
Stargrace’s Wurm Online Friendship Bay trip wraps with forge-side socializing, named animal prizes, and a very relatable ready-to-go-home energy.

Tipa says Captain Sonar was a total hit: turn-based tutorial maps, surprise torpedoes, and a kid-friendly party game nobody checks their phone during.

Wes rounds up early PlayStation 5 emulation buzz, plus more N64 recompilation, fan-translation, and MiSTer save-sync chatter.

Ashley Schofield argues Street Fighter III: 3rd Strike’s parry system is the real masterpiece, even if the roster and balance are gloriously messy.

Scarybooster turns Soulframe into pure battle-poetry, riffing on Wrath, Death, Doom, parries, mounts, and going full wildfire across Midrath.

Ellie finds Viewfinder’s climate-collapse story gives its puzzle-box simulation real weight, making this bleak little puzzler stick harder than usual.

Wilhelm crunches his 2026 gaming spreadsheet: EVE Online leads, but Stardew Valley, Forza Horizon 6, and Windrose are the ones still gaining ground.

Bhagpuss freewheels through a grab bag on memory, vaudeville, and music hall with the kind of digressive charm that makes the whole thing work.

Warner marks turning 70 with a short Sunday reading list on kids, meaning, politics, and Google’s culture, all filtered through birthday perspective.

Bruce Schneier’s weekly squid check-in points to Cape Cod’s beached squid and opens the floor for whatever security news he missed.

Jamie Zawinski responds to a new WordPress exploit with the nuclear option: disable REST, XML-RPC, oEmbed, JSONP, and even WordPress AI support.

Dave Winer shares rss.chat’s new firehose and JSON RSS experiments, then gives devs a pep talk about AI collaboration and protecting interop.

item.title: The Daily Blogroll — Saturday, 18 July 2026
item.description:
John loves Kaz’s frantic tile-based arcade scoring, where upgrades, curses, and cursor-combo spells turn simple movement into dense tactics.

UltrViolet finds Rise of the Ronin mostly average, with the real boss fight being PS5 button memory and that triangle parry.

Sey rounds up Secret Sauce Showcase standouts, from fakeOS horror in SPRKLS.exe to Dreadmoor and Mirage Miracle Quest.

Bhagpuss revisits Palworld for 1.0 and remembers a survival hit that started fun, then drifted into sameness, fiddliness, and chores.

Wilhelm revisits Steam Early Access, weighing playable launches like Valheim, Enshrouded, and Palworld against the usual pay-now, maybe-later gamble.

The Chronicler says March of the Ants: Evolved Edition packs a proper 4X strategy game into 90 minutes, with ants instead of armies.

Jimmy digs into Maxis chasing business simulations after SimCity 2000, including the gloriously uncommercial SimHealth bureaucracy sim.

Anarchae checks back in after family travel with sci-fi reads, Project Hail Mary and old movies, plus Zelda TotK and Fate/GO progress.

Warner is gearing up for a smoke-cleared, grandkid-filled weekend and sounds happily braced for a big day.

Roger finally snaps at Windows 11, arguing its bloat, bugs, and flaky MTP tablet transfers make a new OS feel overdue.

Jamie Zawinski blasts YouTube’s DMCA counter-claim gauntlet after a fair-use Bonnie Tyler parody restoration got rejected and ID verification failed.

Dave Winer says rss.chat now supports feed discovery and argues the social web should embrace full-text posts instead of ActivityPub-style complexity.

item.title: The Daily Blogroll — Friday, 17 July 2026
item.description:
Stargrace heads back to Wurm Online’s Friendship Bay Summer Impalong, a five-day community event full of item improvement, contests, and player generosity.

Kimimi expected Puyo Puyo 20th Anniversary’s twenty rulesets to be a mess, then found a playful puzzle party full of delightful weirdness.

Zack returns from burnout with a broad gaming-industry check-in, led by worries over Sony ending physical game production in 2028.

Wilhelm maps out EVE Online’s Alliance Tournament XXII buildup, a months-long bureaucratic odyssey before the finals finally land around Halloween.

Thomas is hooked by The Alters, praising its existential sci-fi setup and unusually strong choice-and-consequence writing.

Scarybooster remembers Belghast and other gaming bloggers as friends, collaborators, and the people who made the old blogosphere feel like home.

Jeff checks out QuadRF, a Raspberry Pi 5 and FPGA phased-array radio that can track drones and visualize WiFi through walls.

Bruce Schneier highlights Daniel Solove’s case that AI privacy needs company accountability, not just putting personal-data control on users.

Dave Winer keeps iterating on rss.chat with feed icons, server setup tweaks, and even a little Gemini help for images and descriptions.

Aywren announces Blaugust 2026, honoring Belghast’s memory while inviting creators to join a community-first month of blogging.

Brennan gets candid about the daily costs, mixed genres, and decidedly unromantic realities of sustaining a serious writing life.

Jeromai shares an AI-written pep talk about work and worth, then notes Blaugust 2026 is coming and signups are live.

item.title: The Daily Blogroll — Thursday, 16 July 2026
item.description:
Patrick sounds mostly crushed by 4th Edition Underdark, but still delights in Torog and the grotesque secret-hoarders of the Incunabulum.

Magi is all-in on Denshattack!, a pop-punk train trick game that channels Tony Hawk, Jet Set Radio, and pure arcade chaos.

Marc makes the case for Hotel Dusk: Room 215 as a stylish DS graphic adventure worth reviving, linearity complaints be damned.

Sweetie eagerly traces Dragon Quest XII trailer clues that might point back to Dragon Quest IX’s world, and maybe a long-overdue remake.

Wilhelm spun up a solo Stardew Valley save, rediscovered Steam Cloud, and found Switch 2 controller habits followed him to PC.

The Chronicler spotlights Chandragupta as GMT’s Great Battles heads to ancient India, with elephants, chariots, and varied scenarios keeping things lively.

Jamie Zawinski unearthed a 36-year-old VHS of D.O.A.’s 1990 DNA Lounge concert, complete with a YouTube upload for punk-history digging.

Warner reports survival after an all-day, 18-window installation marathon, with plenty of praise for Daniel’s crew and their sweaty professionalism.

Tim Bray wrestles with loving World Cup spectacle while hating FIFA corruption, NFT ticket nonsense, and prices that lock most fans out.

Dave Winer says rss.chat is still an early writing-first bootstrap, adds docs links, and notes his WordPress news site is picking up steam.

Roger pitches Blaugust 2026 as a low-pressure August community for bloggers, podcasters, and creators who want prompts, support, and momentum.

Bhagpuss rallies folks for Blaugust signups, celebrating the blogging festival while honoring Belghast’s memory and the community carrying it forward.
